title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: Standard conjoint analysis tools estimate Average Marginal Component Effects (AMCEs) — causal effect changing single attribute level. AMCEs tell respondents prefer, decide: attribute levels actually attend , ones ignore, order process information. cjdiag fills gap. works level individual attribute levels — aggregated attributes — specific level (e.g., “plans look work”, just “Job Plans” whole) triggers respondent decisions. cjdiag complementary cjoint/cregg (estimate AMCEs). intended workflow : run AMCEs first, run cjdiag diagnose respondents actually made choices.

title: Getting Started with cjdiag
code: library(cjdiag) data(immig)  f <- Chosen_Immigrant ~ Gender + Education + LanguageSkills +   CountryofOrigin + Job + JobExperience + JobPlans +   ReasonforApplication + PriorEntry  rf <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "forest") rf #> Conjoint Random Forest  #> ======================  #>  #> Resolution: levels #> Trees: 500 #> OOB Error: 40.3% #> Observations: 2,000 #> Attributes: 9 #> Levels: 50 #>  #> Top 10 levels by MDA: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 7 #>     rank attribute       level                      mda root_pct class_0 class_1 #>    <int> <chr>           <chr>                    <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans        no plans to look for wo… 13.5      15.4  12.3      7.25 #>  2     2 JobPlans        contract with employer    8.18     11.2   3.70     6.98 #>  3     3 Education       no formal                 7.87      7.4   8.04     2.38 #>  4     4 PriorEntry      once w/o authorization    7.42     10.4   6.87     3.66 #>  5     5 LanguageSkills  fluent English            6.16      8.2   2.71     6.00 #>  6     6 PriorEntry      once as tourist           4.83      2.4   1.61     5.25 #>  7     7 Education       college degree            4.75      6.4   0.153    6.16 #>  8     8 LanguageSkills  used interpreter          4.66      5.6   4.91     1.37 #>  9     9 CountryofOrigin Iraq                      4.15      4.6   3.53     2.15 #> 10    10 Job             janitor                   3.87      3     2.09     3.36 plot(rf, type = "rank", top_n = 20)

title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: Every fit importance object print() method tuned method: print.cjdiag_forest() — header (resolution, ntree, OOB error, observations, attributes/levels) top n rows results table, including mda, root_pct, class_0, class_1. print.cjdiag_tree() — header (cp, root split, depth, terminal nodes) top n rows importance. print.cjdiag_crt() — header (optimal λ, λ-1se, accuracy, attended count) top n rows max_lambda. print.cjdiag_nmm() — header (total pairs, decisive picks) top n levels decisiveness. print.cjdiag_marginal_r2() — per-attribute mean/median R² share respondents R² = 0. print.cjdiag_importance() — shape, dispatched importance object. print methods accept n argument: Without n, print() falls back get_cjdiag_options("print_n") (default 10L). summary() methods exist cjdiag_forest, cjdiag_tree, cjdiag_crt, cjdiag_nmm, cjdiag_importance. return list summary statistics rather printing — useful want numbers without formatted output.

title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: CRT requires profile order assumption (choice probability must invariant side profile shown ). real-world conjoint data don’t satisfy exactly; standard fix flip left/right invert outcome, doubling dataset. data — data frame. outcome — name binary outcome column (0/1). left, right — vectors attribute column names, length. Pairs must align: left[] swapped right[]. Returns data frame 2 × nrow(data) rows: original data followed swapped/inverted copy. Apply cj_fit(..., method = "crt") data isn’t already symmetric. needed forest, tree, nmm, marginal_r2.
code: augment_profile_order(data, outcome,                       left = c("left_attr1", "left_attr2", ...),                       right = c("right_attr1", "right_attr2", ...))

title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: representative end--end session combining several methods:  method asks different question. complementary, competing.
code: # 1. Importance ranking - always start here. rf <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "forest") top_levels <- importance(rf)$results head(top_levels[, c("rank", "attribute", "level", "mda", "root_pct")], 10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>     rank attribute       level                       mda root_pct #>    <int> <chr>           <chr>                     <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans        no plans to look for work 13.5      15.4 #>  2     2 JobPlans        contract with employer     8.18     11.2 #>  3     3 Education       no formal                  7.87      7.4 #>  4     4 PriorEntry      once w/o authorization     7.42     10.4 #>  5     5 LanguageSkills  fluent English             6.16      8.2 #>  6     6 PriorEntry      once as tourist            4.83      2.4 #>  7     7 Education       college degree             4.75      6.4 #>  8     8 LanguageSkills  used interpreter           4.66      5.6 #>  9     9 CountryofOrigin Iraq                       4.15      4.6 #> 10    10 Job             janitor                    3.87      3  # 2. Decision structure - does a single attribute gate the rest? tr <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "tree") tr$root_split   # the gatekeeper, if any #> [1] "JobPlansno.plans.to.look.for.work"  # 3. Decision order - sequential elimination view. nmm <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "nmm",               resp_id = "CaseID", n_boot = 0)  # 4. Per-respondent attendance - heterogeneity diagnostic. mr2 <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "marginal_r2",               resp_id = "CaseID") plot(rf, type = "rank", top_n = 15)

title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: Set seed explicitly (default 42L, make explicit saved analysis script). forest, ntree = 500L plenty stable rankings; ntree = 1000L conservative. crt, lambda grid must extend well beyond 50 — default goes 500 signal-vs-noise separation happens 100 500. Truncating λ = 50 underestimates many levels survive penalization. n_perm = 20L published default; raise 100--500 intend publish individual lambda-survival numbers. nmm, n_boot = 0 returns point estimates ; set 200 (1000) attach bootstrap CIs decisiveness ranks. print() plot() arguments deterministic given fit; cj_fit() uses randomness.

title: CRT / HierNet: Which levels survive a strict signal-vs-noise test?
text: Use method = "crt" want hard attendance test: attribute levels keep non-zero coefficient increasing L1 penalty? Levels vanish quickly penalization noise redundant; levels survive heaviest penalties genuinely drive choices. CRT requires hierNet package. computationally expensive method cjdiag.
code: library(cjdiag) data(immig)  f <- Chosen_Immigrant ~ Gender + Education + LanguageSkills +   CountryofOrigin + Job + JobExperience + JobPlans +   ReasonforApplication + PriorEntry

title: CRT / HierNet: Which levels survive a strict signal-vs-noise test?
text: max_lambda largest λ level still non-zero coefficient. attended boolean derived max_lambda > 0. signal-vs-noise separation happens λ = 100 λ = 500. Stopping grid λ = 50 (lower) common mistake makes nearly every level look “attended”: penalty isn’t yet strong enough eliminate noise. Always extend grid past λ = 200; package default goes 500.
code: crt <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "crt",               lambda_grid = c(5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 500),               n_folds = 3, n_perm = 5) crt #> Conjoint CRT/HierNet Model  #> ==========================  #>  #> Optimal lambda: 5 #> Lambda (1-SE rule): 5 #> Accuracy: 64.8% #> Observations: 2,000 #> Attributes: 9 #> Levels: 50 #> Attended levels: 50 / 50 #>  #> Top 10 levels by MDA: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>     rank attribute            level                       mda max_lambda #>    <int> <chr>                <chr>                     <dbl>      <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans             no plans to look for work 4.14         150 #>  2     2 JobPlans             contract with employer    3.49         100 #>  3     3 PriorEntry           once w/o authorization    1.88         100 #>  4     4 Education            college degree            1.81         100 #>  5     5 CountryofOrigin      Iraq                      1.77          50 #>  6     6 Education            no formal                 1.57         100 #>  7     7 LanguageSkills       used interpreter          1.28          50 #>  8     8 ReasonforApplication escape persecution        1.27          50 #>  9     9 LanguageSkills       fluent English            1.27         100 #> 10    10 ReasonforApplication seek better job           0.910         20

title: Nested Marginal Means: In what order do levels settle choices?
text: Use method = "nmm" (Dill, Howlett & Mueller-Crepon 2024) care order attribute levels settle choices, just static importance. NMM closest match elimination- -aspects (Tversky 1972) level attribute levels. procedure works sequentially: Identify level whose marginal mean deviates 50/50 — decisive level. Remove choice tasks level discriminate (profiles share ). Repeat reduced sample. cumulative plot shows quickly top levels account total decisiveness.
code: library(cjdiag) data(immig)  f <- Chosen_Immigrant ~ Gender + Education + LanguageSkills +   CountryofOrigin + Job + JobExperience + JobPlans +   ReasonforApplication + PriorEntry

title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
text: full results table main output. row one attribute level. columns: rank — position importance ordering. Rank 1 = level work driving choices. attribute — conjoint attribute (question level answers, e.g. immigrant’s reason applying?). level — specific value attribute (e.g. escape persecution). mda — Mean Decrease Accuracy. much forest’s predictive accuracy drops information level shuffled away. Higher = level genuinely shapes choices. Levels mda near zero effectively ignored. root_pct — % trees forest level first split. high value means respondents tend start decision checking level. gatekeeper signal. class_0 — strongly level pushes respondents reject profile. “veto” signal. class_1 — strongly level pushes select profile. “attractor” signal. asymmetry class_0 class_1 reveals direction. class_0 ≫ class_1 means level deal-breaker (e.g. plans look work mostly causes rejection). class_1 ≫ class_0 means level draw (e.g. fluent English mostly causes selection).

Ignored method = "tree". cp Complexity parameter decision tree (default 0.005). Ignored methods. n_boot Number bootstrap iterations NMM confidence intervals (default 0 = bootstrap). Ignored method "nmm". seed Random seed reproducibility (default 42). ... title: Fit Conjoint Diagnostic Model — cj_fit
text: S3 object inheriting cjdiag_fit, subclass depending method (e.g., cjdiag_forest, cjdiag_tree, etc.). objects support print(), plot(), summary(), importance().

title: Fit Conjoint Diagnostic Model — cj_fit
text: "forest" (Random Forest) attribute levels matter choices? Measures much attribute level matters shuffling values checking much worse predictions get (Mean Decrease Accuracy). Also tracks level appears first across hundreds trees (root node rate) — proxy cue respondents check first. Returns class-specific importance (class_0, class_1) showing whether level matters rejection selection. Supports level attribute resolution. "tree" (Decision Tree) respondents structure decisions? Fits single classification tree reveals hierarchical structure choices — attribute acts gatekeeper, attributes matter conditionally, many attributes needed explain choices. Supports resolutions. "crt" (CRT/HierNet) attribute levels survive strict signal-vs-noise test? Applies increasing amounts statistical penalty strip away weak signals (Bien Tibshirani 2014). Levels keep effect even heavy penalization carry signal; levels vanish quickly noise redundant. Levels . "nmm" (Nested Marginal Means) order attributes settle choices? Works attributes one time, starting decisive (Dill, Howlett Mueller-Crepon 2024). step, identifies attribute level strongly tips choices away 50/50, removes tasks level discriminate, repeats. Requires resp_id. Levels . "marginal_r2" (Marginal R-squared) attributes respondent actually use? individual respondent, measures well attribute alone explains choices (Jenke, Bansak, Hainmueller Hangartner 2021). Respondents zero explanatory power attribute likely ignored entirely. Requires resp_id.

title: cjdiag 0.2.1
text: Focuses package smaller set well-supported plots rewrites README around random-forest results table.

title: cjdiag 0.2.1
text: Removed plot(tree_obj, type = "importance"). Decision trees rendered via rpart.plot ; bar chart rpart variable importance removed tree diagnostic bar chart misleading level resolution. Removed plot(nmm_obj, type = "decisiveness") plot(nmm_obj, type = "sample"). Nested marginal means now plots cumulative-explanation curve . underlying decisiveness sample_history columns still available fit object.

title: cjdiag 0.2.1
text: Cumulative rank plots now place label rotated x-axis tick, collisions impossible construction labels dropped. Tick labels show attribute top black level dark grey, separated line break, via ggtext::geom_richtext() (plain rotated-text fallback ggtext unavailable). bottom plot margin scales dynamically longest label. Decision tree plots replace dummy-name labels (e.g. JobPlansno.plans..look..work) attribute: level (e.g. JobPlans: plans look work) via custom split.fun driven fit’s attr_map. Works conjoint data without per-dataset configuration. README: random-forest example shows full results table via knitr::kable() column--column explanations mda, root_pct, class_0, class_1. Importance plot switched bar chart rank plot. README Getting Started vignette now include Estimands Table (estimand / method = / question / output / behavioural assumption / use) users can pick method without reading docs. single Introduction vignette replaced Getting Started overview (cjdiag.Rmd) plus one task-oriented vignette per method (forest, tree, nmm, marginal_r2, crt). Plot help pages now share @family plotting block help pages cross-link. README: added ERC AGAPP funding acknowledgement.

title: cjdiag 0.2.0
text: Initial CRAN release.

title: cjdiag 0.2.0
text: Random forest (method = "forest"): attribute importance via Mean Decrease Accuracy, Mean Decrease Gini, root node appearance rates. Supports level-specific attribute-level resolution. Decision tree (method = "tree"): CART classification tree revealing hierarchical decision structure. Root split identifies gatekeeper attribute. CRT/HierNet (method = "crt"): L1-regularized logistic regression across lambda grid. Lambda path, cross-validation, permutation importance (MDA). Nested marginal means (method = "nmm"): sequential elimination procedure. Decisiveness ranking reveals decision order. Marginal R-squared (method = "marginal_r2"): per-respondent per-attribute importance metric.

title: cjdiag 0.2.0
text: Unified cj_fit(formula, data, method) interface methods. importance() generic methods model types. Plot customization: palette, base_size, label_wrap, attribute.names, level.names, group_by_attribute, full theme override. Three palettes: "default", "colorblind" (Okabe-Ito), "grey". Global options: set_cjdiag_theme(), set_cjdiag_labels(), get_cjdiag_options(). tidy() glance() methods (conditional broom/generics). Bundled immig dataset (Hainmueller & Hopkins 2015).
